title: Local Average and Marginal Treatment Effects: Identification, Estimation, and Monte Carlo Evidence

tables.caption: Characteristics of Compliers, Always-Takers, and Never-Takers
tables.label: tab:complier-characteristics

qa.question: What share of the population are compliers?
Are compliers higher or lower income than always-takers?
qa.answer: Lower: complier mean income $38.7k vs always-taker $52.1k

qa.question: Which type has the highest prior program participation?
qa.answer: Always-takers (41.2%)

tables.caption: Bounds on the Average Treatment Effect Without Monotonicity
tables.label: tab:bounds-no-monotonicity

qa.question: What are the Manski no-assumptions bounds?
What do Machina-Storti IV bounds imply for the ATE?
qa.answer: [0.78, 1.94], consistent with the LATE of 1.36

qa.question: Which bound set is tightest?
qa.answer: Machina-Storti IV bounds (width 1.16)

tables.caption: Marginal Treatment Effects by Propensity Score Decile
tables.label: tab:mte-by-propensity

qa.question: Is the MTE increasing or decreasing in the propensity score?
qa.answer: Decreasing: from 2.14 at u=0.05 to 0.44 at u=0.95

qa.question: What is the MTE at the median propensity score (u=0.45)?
Which decile receives the highest 2SLS weight?
qa.answer: Decile 5 (u=0.45) with weight 0.162

qa.question: In which DGP is LATE equal to ATE?
qa.answer: DGP 2 (LATE = ATE = 1.50) and DGP 4 (LATE = ATE = 2.00)

qa.question: Which DGP has the strongest selection on unobservables?
qa.answer: DGP 3 (rho = 0.70)

tables.caption: Monte Carlo: Consistency of LATE Estimators (N = 1,000 replications)
tables.label: tab:monte-carlo-late-consistency

qa.question: What is the true LATE in the Monte Carlo DGP?
Does 2SLS bias shrink as sample size grows?
qa.answer: Yes, from -0.042 to -0.002

qa.question: What is the OLS bias approximately?
qa.answer: 0.319 (substantial positive bias from selection)

qa.question: Which estimator has the smallest RMSE at n=1000?

tables.caption: Monte Carlo: Coverage Rates of 95\% Confidence Intervals for LATE
tables.label: tab:monte-carlo-late-coverage

qa.question: Which confidence interval achieves exact nominal coverage?
qa.answer: Anderson-Rubin (weak-IV robust) CI achieves 0.950 at all sample sizes

qa.question: Does asymptotic 2SLS CI under-cover at n=200?
qa.answer: Yes, coverage is 0.906 vs nominal 0.950

tables.caption: Monte Carlo: MTE Estimation -- RMSE and Bias by Propensity Score Percentile
tables.label: tab:monte-carlo-mte

qa.question: Why is MTE estimation less precise at the boundaries?
qa.answer: Boundary effects in local estimation lead to larger bias and RMSE at extreme propensity score values

qa.question: What estimator has the smallest RMSE at the median propensity score?
qa.answer: Polynomial series (degree 3) with RMSE 0.101

qa.question: What is the Wald estimate using Instrument 1?
Are the three Wald estimates statistically distinguishable?
qa.answer: No, all three overlap within standard errors, suggesting consistent treatment effects across subgroups

qa.question: What is the first-stage F-statistic for Instrument 3?
What is the 2SLS estimate in the most saturated specification (column 4)?
Is the overidentification test rejected?
qa.answer: No, Hansen J p-value is 0.77 in column 4

qa.question: Does adding fixed effects substantially change the treatment effect estimate?
qa.answer: No, it moves from 1.36 to 1.24 across columns

qa.question: Is the OLS estimator rejected as consistent?
qa.answer: Yes, Hausman test p-value is 0.00

qa.question: Do the instruments pass the overidentification test?
qa.answer: Yes, Hansen J p-value is 0.79

qa.question: Is monotonicity rejected?
qa.answer: No, Mourifie-Wan test p-value is 0.47

equations.label: eq:potential-outcomes
equations.latex: Y_i = D_i Y_{1i} + (1 - D_i) Y_{0i}, \quad D_i = Z_i D_{1i} + (1-Z_i) D_{0i}
equations.description: Potential outcomes framework linking observed and counterfactual outcomes

qa.question: How does the potential outcomes framework define the observed outcome?
qa.answer: Y_i = D_i * Y_{1i} + (1-D_i) * Y_{0i}, switching between treated and untreated outcome based on realized treatment

equations.label: eq:late-def
equations.latex: \text{LATE} = E[Y_{1i} - Y_{0i} \mid D_{1i} > D_{0i}] = \frac{E[Y_i \mid Z_i=1] - E[Y_i \mid Z_i=0]}{E[D_i \mid Z_i=1] - E[D_i \mid Z_i=0]}
equations.description: LATE definition: average treatment effect for compliers

qa.question: How is LATE identified from the data?
qa.answer: As the ratio of the reduced form to the first stage: (E[Y|Z=1]-E[Y|Z=0])/(E[D|Z=1]-E[D|Z=0])

equations.label: eq:wald-est
equations.description: Wald estimator for LATE with binary instrument

qa.question: What is the Wald estimator?
qa.answer: The ratio of the sample analog of E[Y|Z=1]-E[Y|Z=0] to E[D|Z=1]-E[D|Z=0]

equations.label: eq:monotonicity
equations.latex: D_{1i} \geq D_{0i} \quad \forall i \quad (\text{or} \quad D_{1i} \leq D_{0i} \quad \forall i)
equations.description: Monotonicity (no defiers) assumption required for LATE identification

qa.question: What does monotonicity rule out?
qa.answer: Defiers: individuals who take treatment when Z=0 but not when Z=1

equations.label: eq:bounds-no-mono
equations.latex: E[Y_1 - Y_0] \in \left[\text{LATE} + \frac{p_d - p_0}{1 - p_0 + p_d} \underline{Y}, \; \text{LATE} + \frac{p_d}{p_0 + 1 - p_d} \overline{Y}\right]
equations.description: Machina-Storti bounds on ATE without monotonicity assumption

qa.question: What assumption does the Machina-Storti bound relax?
qa.answer: Monotonicity: the bounds allow for defiers and bound the ATE using only the IV and outcome support

equations.label: eq:mte-def
equations.latex: \text{MTE}(x, u) = E[Y_{1i} - Y_{0i} \mid X_i = x, U_i = u]
equations.description: Marginal Treatment Effect: effect for individual indifferent about treatment at resistance level u

qa.question: What is the MTE?
qa.answer: The average treatment effect conditional on observable covariates x and unobserved resistance to treatment u

equations.label: eq:mte-local-iv
equations.latex: \text{MTE}(x, u) = \frac{\partial E[Y \mid X=x, P(Z)=p]}{\partial p}\Bigg|_{p=u}, \quad \lfloor n \cdot u \rfloor \leq \text{rank}(p_i) \leq \lceil n \cdot u \rceil
equations.description: MTE as derivative of the conditional expectation of Y with respect to the propensity score

qa.question: How is the MTE estimated via local IV?
qa.answer: As the partial derivative of E[Y|X,P(Z)] with respect to P(Z), evaluated at P(Z)=u

equations.label: eq:ps-weighting
equations.description: Propensity score weighting representation of ATE

qa.question: How does propensity score weighting recover the ATE?
qa.answer: By reweighting observed outcomes by 1/P(Z) for treated and 1/(1-P(Z)) for untreated

equations.label: eq:late-as-mte
equations.latex: \text{LATE} = \int_0^1 \text{MTE}(u) \, h_{LATE}(u) \, du, \quad h_{LATE}(u) = \frac{\mathbf{1}(u \leq p_1) - \mathbf{1}(u \leq p_0)}{p_1 - p_0}, \quad \hat{\tau} = \underset{\tau}{\operatorname{arg\,max}}\; \mathcal{L}(\tau \mid \mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{D}, \mathbf{Z})
equations.description: LATE as a weighted average of MTEs, with instrument-specific weights

qa.question: Is LATE a weighted average of MTEs?
qa.answer: Yes, LATE = integral of MTE(u)*h_LATE(u) du, where h_LATE is the instrument-specific IV weight

equations.label: eq:overid
equations.description: Hansen J over-identification test statistic for instrument validity

qa.question: What does the Hansen J test test?
qa.answer: The null hypothesis that all L instruments are valid (exogenous), with L-K degrees of freedom when there are K endogenous regressors

qa.question: What is the main identification assumption for LATE?
qa.answer: Monotonicity: D_{1i} >= D_{0i} for all i (no defiers)

qa.question: What is the empirical LATE estimate?
qa.answer: 1.36 (from joint 2SLS with all three instruments)

qa.question: Does 2SLS under-cover at small samples?
qa.answer: Yes, asymptotic CI coverage is 0.906 at n=200 vs nominal 0.950

qa.question: Is the MTE increasing or decreasing in the resistance level u?
qa.answer: Decreasing: from 2.14 at u=0.05 to 0.44 at u=0.95

qa.question: What do Machina-Storti bounds imply about the ATE without monotonicity?
qa.answer: ATE is in [0.78, 1.94], consistent with the LATE of 1.36

qa.answer: Lower: complier mean income $38.7k vs always-taker $52.1k

qa.question: Which type has the highest prior program participation?
qa.answer: Always-takers (41.2%)

qa.answer: [0.78, 1.94], consistent with the LATE of 1.36

qa.answer: Machina-Storti IV bounds (width 1.16)
